attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-year history of severe seasonal rhinitis symptoms from September to November. She reports nasal congestion, rhinorrhoea, and frequent episodes of sneezing that significantly impact her work as a teacher. When she mows the lawn, she notices her legs become red and itchy. She has tried regular oral antihistamines, intranasal corticosteroids, and avoiding outdoor activities, with minimal improvement. She has no other medical conditions and takes no regular medications. Skin prick testing confirms grass pollen allergy.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is pale, boggy nasal mucosa with clear rhinorrhoea. Her lower legs are mildly erythematous with scratch marks. The remainder of the examination is unremarkable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate long-term management option?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with severe seasonal allergic rhinitis that is significantly impacting her quality of life despite appropriate use of first-line therapies. The confirmed grass pollen allergy and failure of conventional treatments make her an excellent candidate for specific allergen immunotherapy.

Allergen immunotherapy involves the administration of gradually increasing doses of allergen extract to induce immunological tolerance. It is the only disease-modifying treatment available for allergic rhinitis and can provide long-lasting benefit even after discontinuation. It is particularly appropriate for patients with severe symptoms who have failed conventional therapy and have a demonstrated allergy to a specific allergen.

Oral prednisolone would provide temporary symptom relief but is not appropriate for long-term management of allergic rhinitis due to its significant side effect profile. It should be reserved for severe exacerbations or emergencies.

Montelukast is a leukotriene receptor antagonist that may be helpful as add-on therapy for allergic rhinitis, but it is unlikely to provide significant additional benefit in a patient who has failed other first-line treatments.

Change of occupation is an extreme measure that should not be necessary with appropriate medical management. While environmental modifications are important, complete avoidance of grass pollen is generally impractical.

Higher dose antihistamines are unlikely to provide significant additional benefit and may increase side effects such as sedation. The patient has already failed appropriate doses of antihistamines combined with intranasal corticosteroids.

Allergen immunotherapy requires careful patient selection, as it involves a significant time commitment (3-5 years of therapy) and carries a small risk of systemic allergic reactions. It should be initiated under specialist supervision, but it offers the possibility of long-term remission of symptoms.
learningPoints: Allergen immunotherapy (desensitisation) is indicated for patients with severe allergic rhinitis who have failed conventional therapy.
Immunotherapy should be initiated under specialist supervision due to the risk of anaphylaxis.
references: Durham SR, Emminger W, Kapp A, Colombo G, de Monchy JG, Rak S, Scadding GK, Andersen JS, Riis B, Dahl R. Long-term clinical efficacy in grass pollen-induced rhinoconjunctivitis after treatment with SQ-standardized grass allergy immunotherapy tablet. J Allergy Clin Immunol. 2010: 125(1).  Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20109743/
Hu W, Katelaris C, Kemp A. Allergic Rhinitis. Australian Family Physician. 2008: 37 (4). Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/getattachment/a5276d14-b5ae-4266-87ad-d251ce3da7e7/200804hu.pdf
